1:
The register of the resettled community opens by looking backward, and the witnesses fasten first on the double statement it makes — that all Israel was enrolled, and that Judah went into exile for her sin. The reformed John Gill reads the opening clause as a note about records rather than about the writer's own preceding chapters: not every tribe has been reckoned by him, and the rest only in part, but an exact account of all Israel had been kept, from which he draws. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees and, following Jarchi, hears in the verse the voice of Ezra the compiler: I have given the genealogies as I found them in a book carried into Babylon when the people were removed for their transgressions, and that book is what I have transcribed in the foregoing chapters. John Wesley adds only that "the book" means the public records, wherein was kept an account of the kingdom and of its several families.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch scrutinize the syntax and correct the old versions. The Septuagint and Vulgate, they say, wrongly joined "Judah" to the preceding words and rendered "the book of the kings of Israel and Judah"; the true sense is that all the Israelites were registered in the book of the kings of Israel, and that Judah, as a separate clause, was carried to Babylon for her unfaithfulness. They reject too the notion of Bertheau that "Israel" here means only the northern tribes, since the very source cited elsewhere as "the book of the kings of Israel" is elsewhere called "the book of the kings of Judah and Israel" — one national chronicle, not a partisan one.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the verse into a lesson in self-judgment. The genealogies, he says, were gathered out of the books of the kings, not the canonical Scripture but an authentic civil record, like a king's rolls among us; and the historian, naming Israel and Judah, will not let their carrying away to Babylon be forgotten. Let that judgment be ever remembered, Henry urges, for warning to posterity to take heed of the sins that brought it; and whenever we speak of any calamity that has befallen us, it is good to add, "it was for my transgression," that God may be justified and clear when he judges. So the witnesses, from three schools, make this backward-looking verse do two things at once: it vouches for the accuracy of the long pedigree just concluded, and it stamps upon the whole restored nation the memory of why it had been scattered.
2:
The list of the returned begins, and the witnesses agree that the four classes named here are the whole fabric of the restored commonwealth. The reformed John Gill takes "the first inhabitants that dwelt in their possessions" to be those who came back first upon the proclamation of Cyrus and reoccupied their former cities, or such as were allotted them. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the fourfold order that had stood ever since the days of Joshua: the priests, the Levites, the common people or simple Israelites, and the Nethinim — the temple-slaves, remnant of the Gibeonites who by their craft had saved their lives and been condemned to this service, as Joshua 9:21 records. In David's time, Clarke supposes, other conquered peoples were added to them, since the Gibeonites' heirs could not do all the drudgery of the sanctuary.

John Wesley presses the theology hidden in the general name "Israelites." It is used, he says, of the common people of both Judah and Israel, restored to them now that the two houses are united in one commonwealth, so that the old names and signs of their unhappy division might be blotted out. Though the body of the ten tribes still lay in captivity, divers of them, at Cyrus's general decree, associated themselves and came up with Judah and Benjamin. The Levites likewise repossessed their cities as they had need and opportunity; and the Nethinims, that order devoted to the service of God's house and of the priests, were given their own places that they might attend their work without distraction.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the structural key that governs the whole chapter and, with it, the sharpest disagreement of these verses. This verse, with the one following, is not the superscription of a register of Jerusalem's inhabitants only, they insist, against Bertheau; it introduces the contents of the entire chapter and answers to the resumption at v.34. And they mark the crucial relation to the parallel: in Nehemiah 11 we have a register of the inhabitants of Jerusalem and of the cities of Judah, whereas this chapter contains only a register of the former inhabitants of Jerusalem. The two lists resemble each other, they grant, just so far as both treat of Jerusalem — the sons of Judah, of Benjamin, the priests and Levites — but no further. The witnesses thus stand the whole later comparison with Nehemiah on this ground: the same source lies behind both, but the Chronicler has drawn from it his own, narrower extract.
3:
The historian names the tribes that peopled the holy city, and the witnesses read the short verse both as plain fact and as the first testing-point of the chapter's relation to Nehemiah. The reformed John Gill observes simply that of Judah and Benjamin were the largest number who went into captivity and returned, so that they naturally furnished the bulk of Jerusalem's resettlers. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke widens the notice: not Judah and Benjamin only, but men of Ephraim and Manasseh took advantage of Cyrus's proclamation to return and mingle with those to whom Jerusalem had belonged — a mixing that was necessary, he adds, to provide population enough for so large a city.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the verse with his characteristic eye on the parallel document. The opening sentence, he notes, is word for word the same as the first half of Nehemiah 11:4; but the next clause, "and some of the children of Ephraim and Manasseh," is not found in Nehemiah at all, and nothing further is said of those two tribes anywhere in this chapter. Far from proving the clause a figment of the Chronicler's invention, Ellicott argues, this only shows that he has used the ordinary freedom of a compiler in transcribing from the fuller document that supplied his materials both here and in Nehemiah. That source dealt with the neighbouring townships as well as with Jerusalem; the city alone is the subject of the Chronicler's extract here.

The witnesses thus converge on a single reading of a verse that might seem the barest of lists. The naming of Ephraim and Manasseh beside Judah and Benjamin is no small thing: it signals, as Wesley had felt at the verse before, that the restored community was Israel entire and not Judah alone, the old breach between the kingdoms healed in the return. And the very detail by which this list departs from Nehemiah — the mention of the two northern tribes — becomes, in Ellicott's hands, evidence not of the Chronicler's carelessness but of his method, a compiler selecting freely from a common register, keeping what served his purpose of showing a reunited people gathered again about the temple, and letting the rest fall to Nehemiah's fuller roll.
4:
The first named settler of Judah is Uthai, and the witnesses trace his line while confronting at once the puzzle that will run through the whole register — its divergence from the parallel roll in Nehemiah. The reformed John Gill notes that Uthai is called Athaiah in Nehemiah 11, though his ancestors are there differently reckoned, and traces his descent through Ammihud, Omri, Imri, and Bani to Pharez the son of Judah. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the whole list as those who returned first with Zerubbabel, the Chronicler meaning to give the names of the earliest returners, while Nehemiah's fuller catalogue takes in those who came afterward.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the man in his place: of the family of Pharez was Uthai, whose descent is carried back to Bani, and they resolve the awkward Hebrew of the pedigree, reading with the Keri "of the sons of Bani." They observe frankly that in the genealogies of Judah in the earlier chapters neither Bani nor Uthai nor any of the ancestors here named is mentioned, and that Nehemiah 11:4 traces Athaiah through wholly other forebears back to Mahalaleel, not one of the five intervening names agreeing with the ancestors of Uthai.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, meets the discrepancy head-on and turns it to instruction. Nehemiah's line runs "Athaiah son of Uzziah, son of Zechariah, son of Amariah, son of Shephatiah, son of Mahalaleel"; Uthai is equivalent to Athaiah, and Imri a contraction of Amariah, but the other names do not coincide. This, he urges, does not prove that Uthai and Athaiah are different clans, for many more than five or six members would be needed to make a complete genealogical stem reaching from post-exilic times back to the tribal patriarch; each writer has simply selected different names out of a longer list. John Wesley says the same in his plainer way, ascribing the diversity partly to the Hebrew custom of giving one person several names, and partly to the change of times — some in Nehemiah being such as returned later and dwelt in the room of, or beside, those named here. The witnesses thus refuse to let the mismatch of names become a contradiction: it is the ordinary looseness of ancient pedigree, an abridged descent from Pharez through whom the royal tribe still ran.
5:
The register turns to a second Judahite house, and at once a knot of the text draws the witnesses together. The verse names "the Shilonites," and the reformed John Gill warns that these are not men of Shiloh, the Ephraimite city, but of the tribe of Judah — either the descendants of one named Shiloni or, more likely, the Shelanites, so called from Shelah the son of Judah; and he cites the Targum, which renders it outright "of the tribe of Shelah." Asaiah the firstborn and his sons, he takes to be the head of that clan.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reason the emendation out in full. The epithet "Shilonite" is surprising, they say, for it would denote a native of the Ephraimite Shiloh, and no tie between the men of Judah and that city can be shown; the older commentators therefore rightly suggested reading "the Shelanite," as in Numbers 26:20, where the family of Shelah, Judah's third son, is so named. The faulty pointing, they judge, arose from the fuller spelling of the word, and the corrected form actually appears in Nehemiah 11:5, where, in place of Asaiah, Maaseiah stands as the descendant of the Shelanite in the seventh generation.

Charles Ellicott confirms the same correction from the versions and presses the sense of the names. "Shilonite" means man of Shiloh, the ancient capital of Ephraim, whereas these verses have to do with Judah, whose three sons who founded sub-tribal divisions were Pharez, Shelah, and Zarah; the clan of Shelah was the Shelanite of Numbers 26:20, and that, supported by the Septuagint and the Targum, is doubtless the true reading. Asaiah, "Jah hath wrought," he adds, is essentially the same name as Maaseiah, "work of Jah," in Nehemiah, where six progenitors are enumerated; and "the firstborn" here means the leading clan, "his sons" its members. The witnesses do not divide but reinforce one another: a single mispointed letter has disguised the Shelanites of Judah as men of Ephraim's Shiloh, and the comparison of Numbers, Nehemiah, the Greek, and the Targum recovers the plain descent from Shelah.
6:
The third Judahite branch is the house of Zerah, and the witnesses attend both to its head and to the number attached to it. The reformed John Gill notes only that Zerah was another son of Judah, but the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the grammar with care. "Of the sons of Zerah, Jeuel" — a name borne by various persons elsewhere — and "their brethren, six hundred and ninety." The plural "their" cannot refer to Jeuel alone, they argue, since he is the head of a father's-house and not a collective; it must reach back over the three heads already named, Uthai, Asaiah, and Jeuel, whose brethren are the other heads of the three Judahite families, exactly as the number in v.9 gathers up all the heads before it.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, compares the parallel and finds the Chronicler again exercising his freedom. The Zarhites are wholly omitted in Nehemiah 11, which reads instead that "all the sons of Perez that dwelt at Jerusalem were four hundred threescore and eight valiant men"; the common source, he infers, must have contained information about the Zarhites as well as their brother clans the Parzites and Shelanites. From the verse before us, he observes, the Zarhites were actually more numerous in Jerusalem than the Parzites, and the six hundred and ninety is the total of the Zarhites only, the number of the other two clans not being given here. The plural "their brethren," he agrees with Keil, plainly hints that Jeuel names a Zarhite clan and not a single man.

So the witnesses read the verse as a small but telling instance of how the two registers relate. Where Nehemiah lumps the descendants of Pharez into one round figure and drops Zerah, the Chronicler counts the Zarhites separately and passes over the Parzite total — each compiler pruning the common list to his own end. The reformed and grammatical-historical voices meet on the substance: three families of Judah, sprung from Pharez, Shelah, and Zerah, resettled the city, and the numbers, however distributed between the two accounts, testify that the royal tribe returned in strength to the place of the throne.
7:
The register crosses now to Benjamin, and the witnesses count the heads of that tribe and weigh them against the parallel. The reformed John Gill notes that these were of the tribe of Benjamin who had gone with Judah into captivity and returned with them, or had dwelt in Jerusalem before. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the structure: four Benjamite heads are named — Sallu, Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam — with the ancestry of the first and fourth given to three generations, of the second only the father, of the third the father and grandfather; and their brethren, "according to their generations," number nine hundred and fifty-six. Comparing Nehemiah 11, they find only one of the four, Sallu, likewise a son of Meshullam though with different forebears, while the three others are replaced by "Sallai, nine hundred and twenty-eight," and by officers named as overseer and as ruler over the city.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, presses the comparison further and toward reconciliation. Nehemiah traces Sallu's ancestry four generations further back; and for "the son of Hodaviah, the son of Hasenuah" he suggests reading "and Hodaviah," or even "and Judah," comparing Nehemiah's "Judah the son of Senuah." The name Hodaviah he explains as an Aramaizing form meaning "thank the Lord." His larger point is that the divergences are textual, not substantial: where the two rolls seem to name different men, corrupt spellings and dropped conjunctions have obscured an underlying agreement.

The witnesses take the verse, then, as one more proof of the two registers' common root. The reformed Gill is content to identify these as Benjamites of the return; the grammatical-historical Keil and Ellicott dig beneath the surface, showing that the same list lies behind both the Chronicler and Nehemiah, worn by copying into apparent disagreement. That Benjamin's returned heads number near a thousand, and that they dwelt in the city beside Judah, is the settled fact all affirm; the differences of name and number are the ordinary bruises of transmission, not rival traditions, and the witnesses will not make of them a contradiction where an abridged and copied pedigree suffices to explain the whole.
8:
Three more Benjamite houses are named — Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam — and the witnesses, chiefly the grammatical-historical, use the verse to work out the tangled correspondence with Nehemiah. The reformed John Gill notes only that these three, with their ancestors, were of Benjamin and settled at Jerusalem, and are met with nowhere else. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the pedigrees — Ibneiah son of Jeroham, Elah son of Uzzi son of Michri, Meshullam son of Shephatiah son of Reuel son of Ibnijah — and reckon these, with Sallu of the previous verse, the four heads whose brethren numbered nine hundred and fifty-six, "all these men chief of the fathers in the house of their fathers."

Charles Ellicott labours the parallel with his usual patience and turns apparent chaos into concord. Ibneiah and the closing Ibnijah are much the same name, "Jah buildeth," that is, maketh offspring; Michri should perhaps be Zichri; and where Nehemiah reads "Gabbai, Sallai," the words may be corrupt, Gabbai perhaps concealing a contracted form of Ibneiah and Sallai arising out of Shallum or Meshullam under the pull of the preceding Sallu. Nehemiah's "Joel son of Zichri" as overseer he equates with "Elah son of Uzzi son of Michri" here, since Joel and Elah may both disguise Elijah; and "Judah son of Hasenuah" with "Hodaviah son of Hasenuah." If these combinations be accepted, he concludes, the list is brought into strict harmony with its parallel — five Benjamite clans named in each, Sallu, Hodaviah, Ibneiah, Joel, and Meshullam. He explains the differing totals, nine hundred and fifty-six here against nine hundred and twenty-eight in Nehemiah, by supposing the two texts refer to slightly different dates, and reads the closing phrase as a subscription: "all these men were chiefs of their respective clans," the proper names standing for collective personalities.

The witnesses thus make the verse a case-study in the Chronicler's method and the versions' wear. Gill lets the obscure names stand as men of Benjamin and no more; the grammatical-historical school, unwilling to leave a contradiction unexamined, reconstructs behind the two rolls a single register of five Benjamite houses, its names contracted, transposed, and miscopied into seeming difference. What all affirm is that these were the chief fathers of Benjamin's returned families, collective clan-heads and not mere individuals, numbered that the restored city might know its own.
9:
The tally of Benjamin's houses closes on a number, and the witnesses use the summary to weigh the whole lay community that has now been counted. The reformed John Gill records that the resident Benjamites amounted to nine hundred and fifty-six, a total he notes to have greatly exceeded the reckoning of Judah put together, so that in the resettled city the smaller tribe outnumbered the greater. The verse then rounds off the roll with the words that all these were chief of the fathers in the house of their fathers — the heads of households, the representative men whose names stood in the register for the clans that gathered behind them.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley meets the difficulty that the parallel register in Nehemiah 11:8 gives the Benjamites as only nine hundred and twenty-eight. He offers two reconciliations without impugning either text: either Nehemiah counts only those whom the lot had determined to dwell at Jerusalem, to whom the Chronicler here adds such as freely offered themselves to the city, or else some who were first placed there had since died or removed upon some occasion, so that the two rolls were simply taken at different moments in a community still settling itself. Both numbers, on his reading, are true records caught at different hours.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the passage, lifts the bare catalogue into honour. Divers are here named, and many more numbered, who were chief of the fathers, and they ought to be remembered with honour as Israelites indeed — men whose spirit God had stirred up to enquire the way to Zion again, when many both of Judah and of Israel stayed behind in Babylon. Henry hears in the very mingling of the tribes the fulfilment of the old promise that the children of Judah and of Israel should be gathered together and come up out of the land, one nation again, as Hosea 1:11 and Ezekiel 37:22 had foretold; trouble drives together those who had been at variance, and the pieces long separated run together again when they are melted in one crucible. The witnesses do not divide. Gill fixes the number, Wesley harmonises it with Nehemiah, and Henry draws the moral; together they let the verse do its quiet work, capping the account of the laity — the Israelites of Judah, Benjamin, and the remnant of the northern tribes — before the historian turns, in the verse that follows, to the priests who came up with the first.
10:
With the priests the register climbs from the laity to the sanctuary, and the witnesses at once distinguish names from persons. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that these were the priests who returned and dwelt at Jerusalem. But the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the true nature of the three names, Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jachin: they do not designate individual men but three of the priestly courses or classes instituted by David according to 1 Chronicles 24, of which Jehoiarib was the first, Jedaiah the second, and Jachin the twenty-first. The whole course, and not one priest, is meant to have dwelt in the city.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, read the verse identically, taking the three names to denote three classes of priests, as the parallel in 1 Chronicles 24:7 confirms, who accordingly resided at Jerusalem. And Ellicott turns the comparison with the parallel to account: Nehemiah 11:10 reads "Jehoiarib son of Jedaiah," which he judges a mistake of the scribe, two coordinate course-names having been wrongly bound into a father-and-son relation — the same kind of copying-error he had traced among the Benjamites, where a list of coordinate clans was misread as a line of descent.

The witnesses thus set the reader on guard for the whole priestly section that follows. To count these as three men would be to misread the register altogether; they are the great sacerdotal divisions, hereditary guilds tracing themselves to David's ordering of the priesthood, and their presence in the restored city meant that the ancient rota of temple service could be resumed. The lesson the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry drew over this section governs the verse: it was the priests' praise that they came up with the first, for who should lead in a good work if not the Lord's ministers, and it was the people's praise that they would not come without them, for who but the priests should keep knowledge and bless the people in the name of the Lord? The naming of the courses of Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jachin is therefore no dry catalogue but the sign that the worship of the second temple was restored upon its old Davidic foundations, the classes returning each to its appointed week of ministry.
11:
The register names the chief priest of the restored sanctuary, and the witnesses gather around both his pedigree and his office. The reformed John Gill untangles the descent: Azariah the son of Hilkiah is traced up through Meshullam — the same as Shallum — through Zadok and Meraioth to Ahitub, the line of the high-priestly house. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify him with the Azariah of 1 Chronicles 6:13, grandfather of the Jehozadak who was led captive to Babylon, and render his title "the prince of the house of God," comparing 2 Chronicles 31:13.

On that title the witnesses agree in substance while ranging the office precisely. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains that the high priest at this time was Jeshua the son of Jozadak, so that Azariah — called Seraiah in Nehemiah 11:11 — was not the high priest but "the ruler of the house," the person next in authority, who had the guard of the temple and command of the priests and Levites; he is the same, Clarke adds, as "the second priest" of 2 Kings 25:18, the sagan or high priest's deputy. John Wesley reads it the same way: not the high priest, but a chief ruler under him. Matthew Henry, of the puritan-evangelical school, had already caught the point that this was the sagan, deputy to Joshua, who perhaps applied himself more diligently to the business than the high priest himself — and had drawn the lesson that in the house of God there must be rulers, not to make new laws but to see the laws of God duly observed by priests as well as people.

Charles Ellicott adds the textual crux and a caution. Comparing 1 Chronicles 6:12, he finds the names agreeing as far as Zadok, but suspects Meraioth and Ahitub have been transposed, or Meraioth omitted earlier; and since Nehemiah reads Seraiah where this verse reads Azariah — the rest of the sentence being verbatim the same — he weighs whether Seraiah should be read here too. Yet if the name is official rather than personal, like the course-names of the previous verse, either Azariah or Seraiah might equally stand for the priestly house intended. So the witnesses leave the man doubly framed: a high-priestly descendant set over the temple as its overseer or sagan, his very name perhaps a house rather than an individual, presiding over the restored worship under Joshua the high priest.
12:
Two more priestly heads are named, and the witnesses trace their lines chiefly to show, once more, how freely the Chronicler has abridged. The reformed John Gill observes that in the genealogy of Adaiah, between Jeroham and Pashur, three names are here omitted — Pelaliah, Amzi, and Zechariah — before the line reaches Pashur the son of Malchijah. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms it from the parallel: Nehemiah 11:12 gives Adaiah's descent in full, inserting just those three names that are wanting here, "another illustration," he says, "of the freedom of the compiler in dealing with these lists." Malchijah, he notes, was the fifth of the twenty-four priestly classes.

The second head, Maasiai son of Adiel, is carried back through Jahzerah and Meshullam to Immer, the sixteenth course of the priests. Here Ellicott works patiently through a thicket of variants. The parallel reads "Amashsai son of Azareel, son of Ahzai, son of Meshillemoth, son of Immer"; Amashsai and Maasiai are variants of one name, both perhaps misspellings of Amasai; Adiel may be a mistake for Azareel; Jahzerah and Ahzai are two forms of the same name; and the Meshullam of this line is either an extra link or a copyist's anticipation of the following name. The line in Nehemiah, he concludes, is originally identical with the present one.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, simply set the two heads in their place, Adaiah son of Jeroham and Maasiai son of Adiel, both traced back to the priestly courses from which they sprang. The witnesses draw from the verse the summary Ellicott states: that at the date of this register three entire courses of the priests, and two clans representing two other courses, besides the ruler or president of the temple named just before, dwelt in Jerusalem. The tangle of dropped names and worn spellings is not, for these commentators, a reason to distrust the record but a window into its making — the Chronicler and Nehemiah drawing on one fuller document, each selecting and each suffering the ordinary corruptions of copying, so that the restored priesthood may be seen returning, course by course and clan by clan, to the service of the house.
13:
The priestly section closes with a great number, and the witnesses divide over how to read both the figure and its grammar. The reformed John Gill takes the plain sense: their brethren, heads of the house of their fathers, were one thousand seven hundred and threescore, a total greater than those of Judah and Benjamin dwelling in Jerusalem put together. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is moved to wonder at it — "what a prodigious number of ecclesiastics to perform the divine service of one temple!" — reckoning some seventeen hundred and eighty able-bodied men, and that independent of the two hundred and twelve porters who served at the gates in v.22.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, however, doubts that so many as seventeen hundred and sixty priestly clans could have dwelt in the holy city, and offers three ways to ease the difficulty: either the phrase "heads of their fathers' houses" belongs to the previous verse and has slipped out of place, or it here means simply "heads of single families," or the number refers to all the guilds and clans of the whole section, verses ten to thirteen, and should be parted from the phrase by a semicolon — this last he thinks probably right. He notes too that the parallel in Nehemiah 11:10-14 gives the priests as one thousand one hundred and ninety-two, and that "for" in "for the work" is wanting in the Hebrew, perhaps a word having fallen out.

On the closing praise of these men the witnesses are one. They were "very able men for the work of the service of the house of God" — literally, as Ellicott and Wesley both mark, "mighty men of valour." John Wesley explains why such a qualification is named of ministers: because the priests might meet great opposition in the discharge of their office, in executing the censures of the law upon impure persons and in preserving sacred things from the touch of forbidden hands. Matthew Henry, of the puritan-evangelical school, had drawn the same note into gospel service: in the house of God there is constant work to be done, and it is well for the church when those are employed in it who are qualified for it, able ministers of the New Testament, mighty men of valour for a work that in this critical juncture, newly come out of Babylon, required great courage and vigour of mind as well as strength of body. So the witnesses leave the verse counting a numerous and valiant priesthood — its exact tally disputed against Nehemiah, its grammar debated — but its whole force bent to show that the restored temple was served by men fit, in strength and in spirit, for the sacred charge.
14:
The register turns to the third order, the Levites, and the witnesses, led here by a long meditation of Matthew Henry, read the whole coming section as a portrait of religion restored to good order. The reformed John Gill names the first Levite, Shemaiah, with his ancestors Hashub, Azrikam, and Hashabiah, of the sons of Merari. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the verse is word for word the same as its parallel, save that here Shemaiah is deduced ultimately from the clan of Merari, while Nehemiah 11:15 adds one more ancestor, Bunni, after Hashabiah and omits the phrase "of the sons of Merari."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the bare name into a survey of the whole restored service. They had smarted, he says, under the former neglect and the late want of ordinances, and so were now zealous to set up the worship of God; and here he marks the good posture into which their affairs were put. Before the temple was built they made use of the plain and movable tabernacle — those who cannot yet reach a temple must be thankful for a tabernacle and make the best of it, and never let God's work be left undone for want of a place to do it in. In allotting to priests and Levites their several employments they had an eye to the model drawn up by David and Samuel the seer, reviving after long interruption the ancient landmarks of v.22. Most Levites dwelt at Jerusalem, though some in the villages, coming up in their weekly courses; many served as porters at the gates, a seemingly mean office the psalmist yet preferred to dwelling in the tents of wickedness. Every one, Henry insists, knew his charge — some over the ministering vessels, some over the fine flour, wine, and oil, some over the anointing oil, some over the shewbread — for in God's house, as in other great houses, the work is done well when every one knows the duty of his place and makes a business of it: God is the God of order, but that which is everybody's work will be nobody's.

So the witnesses set this first Levite at the head of a section that Henry reads almost as a sermon on ordered ministry. Gill and Ellicott fix the man and his Merarite descent and the small divergence from Nehemiah; Henry supplies the theology, that the temple with its day-and-night praise was a figure of the heavenly one where they rest not day nor night from praising God, so that the dry allotment of Levitical duties becomes an image of the church, and of heaven, where each has his charge and all together serve.
15:
Four more Levites are set down, and the witnesses trace the most notable of them back to the great singer of David's court. The reformed John Gill lists Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, and Mattaniah, and follows the pedigree of the last through Micah and Zichri up to Asaph, the singer of David's day and a descendant of Gershon, the firstborn of Levi; the first of the four, Bakbakkar, he thinks with Hillerus to be the same man as the Bakbukiah named among the returned Levites in Nehemiah 11:17.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the same descent — Mattaniah the son of Micah, the son of Zichri, the son of Asaph — and gather in the names that immediately follow as well: Obadiah the son of Shemaiah, traced through Galal to Jeduthun and so a Merarite, and Berechiah the son of Asa, the son of Elkanah, who dwelt in the villages of the Netophathites round about Jerusalem. The two witnesses agree in reading the list not as a string of isolated individuals but as a roll of the heads of the several musical houses, each guild sprung from one of the great singers whom David had set over the service of praise.

The verse opens, the witnesses feel, upon the singers, the most honoured of the Levitical guilds, and its weight lies in the names to which it anchors them. That Mattaniah should be reckoned from Asaph is no small matter, for Asaph stood at the head of the temple music David had ordained, appointed with Heman and Jeduthun over the service of song in 1 Chronicles 25:1; and the return of his line to Jerusalem meant the return of the song of the house itself. Keil and Delitzsch's care to sort the descents — one branch from Asaph the Gershonite, another from Jeduthun the Merarite — shows the Chronicler naming the founders of the restored choirs; so that the reader who recalls the singers of the coming subscription, kept at their charge day and night in v.33, meets here the very men appointed to lead them. The sons of Asaph are come back to Zion, that the courts of the rebuilt temple might not stand mute, but be filled again, morning and evening, with the praise their fathers had been set apart to raise.
16:
A single Levite occupies this short verse, and the witnesses, few here, read him as one of the guild-heads who dwelt not in the city but in the villages round about it. The reformed John Gill notes only that Obadiah the son of Shemaiah is the same who is called Abda the son of Shammua in the parallel, the two names being variant forms of one man. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the fuller descent they had gathered at the previous verse: Obadiah the son of Shemaiah, the son of Galal, the son of Jeduthun — and so of the Merarite line of the singers — and beside him Berechiah the son of Asa, the son of Elkanah, who dwelt in the villages of the Netophathites, the hamlets near Bethlehem where a company of the Levites had their homes.

The witnesses take the notice of the Netophathite villages as the significant thing in so bare a verse. Not all the Levites lodged within Jerusalem; some, as the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry observed in his survey of the chapter, dwelt in the villages, perhaps because there was not yet room for them in the city, yet they were no less employed in the service of the sanctuary, coming up in their courses from time to time. The song of the house, then, was borne by men who kept their farms and homes in the country and came up to serve their appointed weeks — a picture, the witnesses feel, of a ministry rooted in ordinary life and yet devoted to the worship of God.

That this Berechiah and Obadiah should be numbered here, though they lived outside Jerusalem, shows the care of the register to omit none of the Levitical houses. The Chronicler is not counting only the residents of the city but the whole body of returned Levites whose duty lay at the temple, wherever their dwelling. Gill's small note that Obadiah is the Abda of the parallel, and Keil's tracing of his line to Jeduthun, together fix him as one more head of the singers; and the mention of the Netophathite villages quietly records that the servants of the sanctuary were spread through the countryside about the holy city, a settled and ordered ministry drawn up, week by week, to the house of the Lord.
17:
The register comes to the gatekeepers, and the witnesses read the four names at its head as the chiefs of a hereditary body of temple-wardens. The reformed John Gill calls them simply the porters, the keepers of the gates of the tabernacle. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, comparing Nehemiah 11:18-19, finds there only Akkub and Talmon "and their brethren that kept the gates," one hundred and seventy-two in number, with Shallum not appearing and Ahiman perhaps arisen out of the following Hebrew word for "their brethren." He gathers from Nehemiah 12:25-26 that Meshullam — that is, Shallum — Talmon, and Akkub kept ward at the storehouses of the temple gates in the days of Joiakim, Nehemiah, and Ezra, and draws the conclusion that governs the whole section: the names of the porters represent families or guilds which held the hereditary charge of the gates.

Ellicott presses that insight into a general law of the register. All the Levitical functions, he observes, appear to have descended in the same families from father to son, like the various civil offices of the Roman empire; and tradition ascribed the entire arrangement to David, the second founder of the national worship. The name Shallum bears this out, for the closing clause "Shallum was the chief" really belongs, he argues, to the next verse, and should read "Shallum is the chief even unto this day in the king's gate, on the east side." Shallum, meaning "recompense," is the same as Shelemiah in 1 Chronicles 26:14 and Meshelemiah, "Jah recompenseth," in 1 Chronicles 26:1; and the very fact that Shallum is spoken of as warder both in David's day and in the post-exilic age proves that a guild or clan, not a single individual, is in question.

The witnesses find in the eastern station of Shallum a note of dignity. That gate, Ellicott observes, was the post of honour and the royal entry — the king's gate — whose old name would naturally be kept in the restored temple, as Ezekiel 46:1-2 attests. So the humble office the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had honoured, keepers of the thresholds who would rather sit at the door of God's house than dwell in the tents of wickedness, is shown to be no mean thing but an ancient and hereditary trust, its chief guild-heads sprung from Korah and stationed, as in David's day, about the four sides of the house, with Shallum's clan holding the honoured eastern gate through which the king came in.
18:
The verse fixes Shallum's station at the king's gate on the east, and the witnesses read it both as a topographical note and as a quiet clue to the register's date. The reformed John Gill takes it plainly: the porters waited at the gate through which the king went into the temple, on the east side, and were placed there after the captivity in the same order as before. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and John Wesley agree that the king's gate was so called because the kings of Judah went up to the temple through it; Wesley adds that under this one gate the historian comprehends all the rest, likewise guarded by these porters, who kept their watches successively "according to the courses" into which the Levites were distributed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fasten on a point of chronology that the phrase betrays. "And until now is he in the king's gate eastward" must refer, they say, to Shallum, and not to all the doorkeepers, since these kept guard on all four sides; and the remark "until now is Shallum watcher" presupposes the existence of the temple at the time the register was drawn, and so points to a pre-exilic origin for the document. They record the objection of Bertheau, that the name "king's gate" might have survived into post-exilic times, and grant it must in general be admitted — yet it could only stand if it were first proved that Shallum lived after the exile. So the little phrase "until now" becomes evidence, for Keil, that the Chronicler has here incorporated an older, pre-exilic list.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, draws out the archaic colouring of the description. "They were porters in the companies of the children of Levi" he would render "they are warders for the camps of the sons of Levi," recalling Numbers 3:23 and following, where the Levites are prescribed to encamp on the four sides of the tabernacle. The primitive terminology, he explains, is deliberately used to convey that the Levitical wardership of the temple went back historically to that of the Mosaic sanctuary — the gatekeepers of the second temple standing where the tribe of Levi had once pitched about the tent in the wilderness. The witnesses thus make the verse do more than mark a gate: it binds the restored watch to its Mosaic root, and, in the eyes of the grammatical-historical school, quietly discloses that the record itself reaches back beyond the exile to the days when the king still went up through his own gate to the house of God.
19:
Shallum's descent and office are set out at length, and the witnesses read the verse as the register's reach back into the most ancient days of the sanctuary watch. The reformed John Gill traces his pedigree up to Ebiasaph the son of Korah, marking him a Korahite. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch expand the whole: Shallum, descendant of Kore, of Ebiasaph, of Korah, and his brethren of his father's house — the Korahites — were over the work of the service, keepers of the thresholds of the tent, that is, of the temple, which by ancient custom was still called "tent" because God's house had once been a tabernacle; and his fathers, the verse adds, "were by the encampment of Jahve, guardians of the entrance," so that the writer goes back into the ancient time when Shallum's forefathers of the house of Ebiasaph had held the office of guarding the way into the sanctuary.

Charles Ellicott sets the same lineage beside 1 Chronicles 26:1, where Meshelemiah son of Kore appears as a guild of warders under David, and explains Ebiasaph, "the Father hath gathered," as a fuller form of Asaph. The "host of the LORD," he insists with Keil, is not an army but "the encampment of Jehovah," the tabernacle or tent of meeting, which had a single entrance over which the house of Shallum stood guard; and he notes that 2 Chronicles 31:2 applies the same archaic nomenclature, "the gates of the camps of Jehovah," to the temple in Hezekiah's day. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the duty of these keepers of the entry with Jarchi: their business was to suffer no person but the priests to approach the tabernacle during the performance of the sacred service.

John Wesley draws the verse to its edifying point. The tabernacle here means the tent of old, before the temple was built; the "fathers" are the Kohathite forefathers of Shallum; and the "host" recalls the days when Israel, encamped in military order about the tabernacle in the wilderness, had these men set as guardians of the entrance — the "entry" being the way of the veil, so called because then there were as yet no gates. The whole meaning, Wesley says, is that all things were now restored to their primitive order, the several persons taking up the very offices their ancestors had held before them. So the witnesses unite in reading the verse as a bridge across the centuries: the Korahite guild that kept the door of the wilderness tent keeps now the thresholds of the restored temple, the watch of the sanctuary reaching in one unbroken line from the camp of Moses to the gates of the second house.
20:
The register pauses on a great name of the past, and over one clause the witnesses openly divide. The verse recalls that "Phinehas the son of Eleazar was the ruler over them in time past," and adds, "and the LORD was with him." The reformed John Gill understands the ruler to be Phinehas the priest of the days of Moses, who succeeded his father Eleazar in the oversight of the Levites, though he notes that some, with Kimchi, would make him only a Levite of the same name; the words "in time past," Gill thinks, fit the famous Phinehas well. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree that Phinehas was prince over the keepers not as high priest but during the high-priesthood of his father Eleazar, in the time of Joshua, just as Eleazar under Aaron had held the oversight of the guardians of the holy place as "prince of the princes of Levi" in Numbers 3:32.

On the closing words the disagreement surfaces. Keil and Delitzsch deny that "the LORD was with him" is a historical remark, for then a conjunction would stand before it, as in 1 Chronicles 11:9; rather it is a blessing — "Jahve be with him" — uttered in reference, they think, to the covenant of peace God made with Phinehas and his seed in Numbers 25:11-13. Charles Ellicott reads it the same way and draws from it a striking inference: "the LORD be with him!" is a pious ejaculation such as the later Jews used in speaking of a departed worthy, and it is of interest, he says, as indicating a belief in continued existence after death, comparing 1 Chronicles 22:11 and v.16. Against this the plain reading of Gill and the Authorized Version takes the clause as a statement of fact, that the LORD was indeed with Phinehas in his rule.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, drawing on the Targum, keeps to the historical sense and enriches it: Phinehas was ruler over them from ancient times, from the day the tabernacle was set up in the wilderness, "and the Word of the Lord was his assistant." So the witnesses leave the verse framed by a genuine question — whether the sacred writer records that God was with Phinehas, or breathes a benediction over his memory. Yet beneath the difference they share a common reverence: the ancient watch of the sanctuary was once presided over by Phinehas, the zealous priest of the covenant of peace, and the historian names him, whether in fact or in prayer, as a pattern of the God-blessed guardianship that his successors in the porters' office were called to continue.
21:
A second warder of the past is named, and the witnesses are pulled between the Mosaic age and the Davidic. The verse calls Zechariah the son of Meshelemiah "porter of the door of the tabernacle of the congregation," and the reformed John Gill judges that, though some refer it to the days of David, it rather respects one after the Babylonish captivity, whose office nonetheless kept the order established under David. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, by contrast, reads the verse as an abrupt return "from the Mosaic to the Davidic age": Zechariah, he says, had charge of the north gate under David in 1 Chronicles 26:12, and the title should be rendered "a doorkeeper of the tent of meeting," its relation to the porters of v.17 being, he admits, indeterminate.

Keil and Delitzsch feel the awkwardness of the verse most keenly. It is quite unconnected with what precedes, they note, the conjunction being omitted, and its contents present real difficulty. This Zechariah can only be the firstborn of Meshelemiah mentioned in 1 Chronicles 26:2, who lived in the time of David, and to whom, when David divided the porters into classes, there fell by lot the watch toward the north in 1 Chronicles 26:14. But why he alone should be singled out here they cannot certainly say; perhaps, they suggest, he is named as the man who, before David's division of the Levites was carried through, had charge of the porters' service at the tabernacle.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes him for a chief porter in the time of David, keeper of the door that led out of the priests' court into the tabernacle where the ark was placed; and he draws the pastoral note that the section keeps inviting. Before the temple was built they had a mean and movable tent, which they used in the meantime; those who cannot yet have a temple, Wesley urges, let them be thankful for a tabernacle and make the best use of it, and never let God's work be left undone for want of a place to do it in. So the witnesses leave the verse suspended between two ages — a name that seems to reach back to the tent of meeting yet plainly belongs to the Davidic ordering of the gates — and united only in this, that the watch of the door, whoever kept it and in whatever age, was an office of ancient dignity and unbroken trust, carried from the movable tent through David's arrangement down to the porters of the restored house.
22:
The historian numbers the gatekeepers and traces their institution to the earliest days of Israel's ordered worship, and the witnesses attend both to the figure and to the surprising names behind it. The reformed John Gill takes the two hundred and twelve as the number fixed in the days of David, which might not be fewer. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads it as the number of warders at the epoch of which the source is writing, and ranges it among the parallels: Nehemiah 11:19 makes the porters one hundred and seventy-two, Ezra 2:42 says one hundred and thirty-nine returned with Zerubbabel, and under David the warders were ninety-three in 1 Chronicles 26:8-11. The registration "in their villages," he explains, refers to the ancestors of these men, for guilds and corporations do not die.

The clause that most exercises the witnesses is that David and Samuel the seer "did ordain them in their set office." Keil and Delitzsch feel its difficulty: if the verse continues the enumeration begun at v.17, so that the reflections of the preceding verses are a parenthesis, then the two hundred and twelve are the doorkeepers in active service when the register was drawn — yet the statement that David and Samuel appointed them does not readily harmonize with so late a date. Ellicott meets the same knot: no mention is made elsewhere of Samuel's part in arranging the Levitical service, and Samuel died before David's accession in 1 Samuel 25:1; yet tradition associated him with David in the work of religious reform, and the statement may be held true in spirit if not in the letter.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley resolves the difficulty by dividing the work between the two men. In the times of the judges, he says, there was much disorder, the Levites coming to the tabernacle promiscuously as inclination or occasion brought them; but Samuel, observing how greatly they had increased, began to think of establishing order in their ministration, and these intentions, communicated to David, were revived and perfected by the king once he was peaceably settled on his throne. So the witnesses leave the verse with a body of gatekeepers, two hundred and twelve strong, dwelling in their villages and enrolled by genealogy, whose office reached back — whether in strict fact or in the spirit of a long tradition — to Samuel and David together, the seer beginning and the king completing the order of the sanctuary's watch.
23:
The charge of the gatekeepers is declared hereditary, and the witnesses read the verse as binding the office of David's day to the restored temple across the whole span between. The reformed John Gill states it simply: those appointed in David's time to watch the gates had their posterity succeed them in the office, for it was hereditary — they and their children kept the oversight of the gates of the house of the LORD. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render "they and their sons were at the doors of the house of Jahve, of the tent-house," and explain that the phrase "house of the tabernacle" is added to "house of the LORD" precisely so that the latter might not be confined to Solomon's temple, the watch reaching back to the tent; the doorkeepers stood, they add, "according to the four winds," in the arrangement of 1 Chronicles 26:14.

On what "the house of the tabernacle" means the witnesses differ slightly. Charles Ellicott glosses it briefly — "namely, the house of the tabernacle, for the temple was not built in David's day" — reading the phrase as a reminder that in the age when the office was framed there was as yet no temple, only the tent. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws a sharper distinction: the tabernacle here is not the tent David set up in Jerusalem for the ark, but the more solemn tabernacle that Moses had made by God's express command, which in David's time stood at Gibeon and where God was worshipped until the temple was built. Where Ellicott and Keil are content to let the word cover the pre-temple sanctuary in general, Wesley identifies it specifically with the Mosaic tent at Gibeon.

Beneath that small divergence the witnesses agree on the verse's weight. "By wards," or, as Ellicott renders, "for watches," and "by turns or courses," as Wesley has it, the keeping of the gates was parcelled out among the guilds in orderly rotation; and the point that all press is continuity. The very men who kept the doors of the tent, and their sons after them, keep now the gates of the restored house; the office did not lapse in the exile but was taken up again by the same families, so that the watch of the sanctuary runs unbroken from Moses's tabernacle, through David's ordering, to the second temple — a hereditary trust that the return did not create but resumed.
24:
The porters are stationed to the four points of the compass, and the witnesses read the brief verse as a picture of the sanctuary watched on every side. The reformed John Gill notes the four quarters, and adds from the Targum that there were four-and-twenty wards in all. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it "to the four winds used the warders to stand," and explains the historical development behind the arrangement: the keepers stood on the four sides of the tent of meeting, and from the age of Solomon on the four sides of the square enclosure of the temple — the fourfold watch carried over from the tabernacle to the house.

Keil and Delitzsch, taking this verse with the next, set the four-quartered watch within the weekly rhythm of the service. The porters stood toward the east, west, north, and south, while their brethren in the villages were bound to come up "after seven days, from time to time," the seventh day being the sabbath of the week, on which each class in its order took charge of the duties. The infinitive of obligation, they note, expresses that this coming up was a bounden duty and not a matter of choice, the country Levites relieving those on station in a fixed rotation.

The witnesses find in the four quarters more than a matter of arrangement. That the house of God was guarded on every side, east and west and north and south, spoke of a sanctuary hedged about and kept, none of its approaches left unwatched; and the ordering of the watch "to the four winds" recalled, as Ellicott's history shows, the encampment of Levi about the wilderness tent, the same fourfold guard that had kept the tabernacle now keeping the temple. The reformed and grammatical-historical voices converge without dispute: the gatekeepers were disposed on all four sides of the holy place, in an order reaching back to Moses and formalized under David and Solomon, so that the restored worship stood, as of old, ringed continually by its appointed keepers, the door of God's house open on every side yet guarded on every side.
25:
The verse provides for the country Levites who came up in their courses, and the witnesses read it as a glimpse of a ministry rooted in ordinary village life. The reformed John Gill notes only that these brethren dwelt in the villages assigned them. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the picture: the families of the temple-warders, like those of the singers, lived on their farms in the villages round Jerusalem and came up for their duties in weekly rotation, as Nehemiah 12:29 shows; "after seven days" means every seventh day, that is, on the sabbath, when each class entered on its charge.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley expounds the mechanism of the relief in his plain way. The village Levites were to come from their several homes to the place of worship; every seventh day the courses were changed, and the new-comers were to tarry until the next sabbath day; and they came up "to be with them," that is, with the chief porters, who always abode at the place of God's worship. So a fresh company arrived each sabbath to serve its week beside the resident chiefs, and returned to its village when the next relief came.

The witnesses take from this arrangement a lesson already sounded in the section. The service of the sanctuary was borne not by a caste withdrawn from common life but by men who kept farms and homes in the country and were drawn up, week by week, to the house of God — a rhythm the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had noted with approval, that they came "after seven days from time to time," each having his week's attendance in his turn. The ordinary and the sacred were thus woven together: the same men who tilled the villages round Jerusalem stood, on their appointed sabbaths, at the gates of the temple. Gill, Ellicott, and Wesley agree on the plain fact and its quiet beauty — a ministry ordered by sevens, the country brethren relieving the resident chiefs in unbroken succession, so that the watch was never without keepers and no house of Levi was left without its share in the service of God.
26:
The four chief porters are distinguished from the rest, and the witnesses mark both their permanence and their charge over the temple's chambers and treasuries. The reformed John Gill explains that these four, set over all the two hundred and twelve, were never changed nor went down into the country villages, but were always upon the spot and in their office, superintending the rest. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the difficult Hebrew "for in fixed position, or trust, were they, the four heroes of the warders," the same four apparently named in v.17; the temporary chiefs of the warder-guilds abode in the temple, he says, while the mass of their members was settled in the neighbouring villages and occupied with pastoral pursuits.

Keil and Delitzsch describe the trust committed to these resident chiefs: they were "over the chambers and treasuries of the house of God," the cells and treasure-rooms of the sanctuary. Ellicott, attending closely to the structure, argues that this statement really belongs with the following verse and that the account of the warders proper ends with the words "they are the Levites" — the Levites, that is, par excellence; he would transfer the paragraph-mark to v.28, from which point to v.34 the register reviews the other special charges of the Levites. Whether the clause be read with this verse or the next, the witnesses agree on its sense: the chief porters had the oversight of the sacred store-rooms, where, as the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley notes, the holy utensils and other treasures belonging to the temple were kept.

The witnesses find in this the reason the four could not rotate away like their brethren. A trust so weighty — the chambers and treasuries of the house of God — required men constantly upon the spot, that they might both oversee the inferior porters and keep safe the sacred stores. So the chief porters stood apart from the weekly courses, resident and unchanging, the fixed guardians about whom the shifting village companies gathered. Gill's plain observation and Ellicott's careful parsing meet in the same picture: a small body of permanent wardens, mighty men of the guilds, entrusted with the treasure and the oversight of the whole watch, while the greater number served their sabbaths and returned to their farms — the settled heart of a rotating ministry.
27:
The chief porters keep their station through the night and open the house at dawn, and the witnesses read the verse as the fitting close of the gatekeepers' charge. The reformed John Gill pictures them lodging in chambers on the outward wall about the house of God, that they might be near to do their office. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch expand the reason: they passed the night around the house of God because the care of it, the watch over it, was committed to them, and "the opening thereof every morning pertained to them" — they had to unlock the door each morning, the word for "opening" being the key that recurs in Judges 3:25 and Isaiah 22:22.

Charles Ellicott reads it identically, recapitulating the main duty of the Levitical warders: they used to pass the night in the places round the house of God, for upon them was the ward, and they were over the opening — the key — every morning. The whole daily office of the gatekeepers is here gathered into a single sentence: to lodge about the sanctuary through the dark hours, to guard it while the city slept, and at first light to unlock its doors for the worship of the day.

The witnesses feel the quiet dignity of so homely a duty. It was no mean thing, as the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had said of the porters' office, to keep the charge of the house of God; and here that charge is shown at its most constant — men who did not go home at nightfall but lay about the walls of the sanctuary, because the keeping of it never ceased. The reformed and grammatical-historical voices meet without dispute on the picture: the chief keepers ringed the house through the night and opened it every morning, so that the temple was never for an hour unwatched, and its gates swung wide each dawn by the hands of those who had guarded them till day. The near lodging that Gill notes and the morning key that Keil and Ellicott stress together make the gatekeeper the first servant of the house to wake and the last to sleep, his whole life ordered about the door he was set to keep.
28:
The register turns from the gates to the sacred stores, and the witnesses read the charge of the ministering vessels as a study in scrupulous exactness. The reformed John Gill explains that certain Levites had the care of the vessels which the priests used in sacrificing, bringing them to the priests and returning them to their proper places, and counting them "in and out by tale." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take these to be the costly vessels — the golden cups for the libations and the like — brought from the treasure-chamber only for a short time for use in the service, carried in according to their number and, after being numbered again, carried forth.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, dwells on the word "tale" and the discipline it names. The sacred vessels of gold and silver were counted when brought out of the store-rooms and when replaced, to make sure that none was purloined, a care he compares with the weighing of the vessels in Ezra 8:20 and following. "Tale" means reckoning or number, and the verse says literally that "by number they used to bring them in, and by number they used to take them out." Every holy vessel was accounted for at each use, entered and returned under a tally.

The witnesses find in this minute bookkeeping something more than administration. The vessels of the sanctuary were holy, and their keeping was a sacred trust that admitted no carelessness and no loss; to number them in and out was to guard the honour of the house of God against even the possibility of theft or neglect. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had gathered the point into his survey of the chapter: some were entrusted with the plate, the ministering vessels, to bring them in and out by tale, and in God's house, as in other great houses, the work is done well when every one knows the duty of his place and makes a business of it. So the reformed and grammatical-historical voices meet on the same lesson: the God of order was served by men who counted every cup, and the exactness that might seem mere pedantry was in truth reverence — the sacred things handled with a care proportioned to their holiness, delivered and restored under a reckoning that let nothing of the LORD's be lost.
29:
The charge widens from the costly vessels to the whole furniture and stores of the daily service, and the witnesses read the verse as a catalogue of the sanctuary's provisioning. The reformed John Gill notes that some were appointed to oversee the vessels and all the instruments of the sanctuary, which were committed to their care, delivered out on occasion and seen safely returned when their use was done. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch distinguish these from the treasure-vessels of the previous verse: here are others set over the vessels in general, and over all the holy vessels used in the daily sacrificial service, and over the fine flour, the wine, the oil, the frankincense, and the spices required for the meat and drink offerings and for the holy perfumes, comparing Exodus 25:6.

Charles Ellicott reads it the same way, marking that these were the ordinary vessels, all those used in the daily service, together with the supervision of the stores of flour, wine, oil, incense, and spicery, which were the adjuncts of the meat-offerings and libations, and of the holy unguents of Exodus 25:6. The verse thus opens a view of the temple as a great household with its stewards, its stores of grain and drink and fragrant gums laid up and dispensed by appointed keepers, that the altar might never want for what its daily worship required.

The witnesses take from the long inventory the same governing thought that has run through the section. The service of God was not left to improvisation; every kind of holy thing — the vessels of the altar, the fine flour of the offerings, the wine of the libations, the oil, the frankincense, the spices of the perfume — had its own overseers, that all might be ready to hand and none misused or lost. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's note presides here too: every one knew his charge, some over one kind of store and some over another, for that which is everybody's work will be nobody's, and God is the God of order. So the reformed and grammatical-historical voices, differing in nothing, leave the verse as a picture of worship sustained by careful stewardship — the daily sacrifice upheld by a settled commissariat of Levites, each set over his own portion of the holy stores, that the house of God might be served without lack and without disorder.
30:
A parenthesis marks off one task as the priests' alone, and the witnesses agree with one voice on the reason. The verse says that some of the sons of the priests made the ointment of the spices, and the reformed John Gill draws the sharp line it implies: though the Levites had the care of the spices, they might not make the ointment; only the priests could do that. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the ointment as the holy anointing oil of Exodus 30:23-25, whose compounding was accordingly part of the priest's duty and not the Levite's.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the prohibition at its full weight: only the priests were permitted to make this ointment, and all others were forbidden to do so on pain of death, as Exodus 30:34-38 lays down. John Wesley, of the same school, puts it plainly — though the Levites were entrusted with the keeping of this ointment, none but the priests could make it. Charles Ellicott confirms the distinction as the very point of the verse's parenthesis: the Levites had charge of the stores of spicery, but only the priests might lawfully prepare the holy ointment and oil wherewith the sacred tent, the ark, the table, and the rest were anointed, according to Exodus 30:23-29.

The witnesses find in this small reservation a large principle of the sanctuary's order. The keeping of the spices and the compounding of the holy oil were divided by a line no zeal might cross: the Levite might guard the ingredients, but the consecrated anointing oil, so holy that its unauthorized imitation was death, could be mixed only by the consecrated hand. Here, the commentators feel, is the whole logic of the graded service made visible in a single ointment — Levite and priest each with his own bound, the one to keep and the other to make, and neither to usurp the other's office. That the reformed, grammatical-historical, and wesleyan-arminian voices should agree so exactly is itself a witness to the plainness of the law: in the house of God the holiest tasks were fenced about, and the fragrance of the anointing oil rose only from hands that God himself had set apart to compound it.
31:
A single Levite is named for a single homely task, and the witnesses read in it the same principle of ordered and hereditary charge. The reformed John Gill identifies Mattithiah, one of the Levites, as the firstborn of Shallum the Korahite — the very warder-clan already met at the gates. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe his office: he was set "on good faith," in a trust, over the panbakings, the pastry for the meat-offerings, whose preparation he was to watch; the added phrase "one of the Levites" stands in deliberate contrast to "the sons of the priests" of the previous verse, marking that this was a Levite's work, not a priest's.

Charles Ellicott renders "the things that were made in the pans" as the pancakes, or baked cakes, and notes that the Hebrew word occurs here only, its meaning fixed by the related term for a baking-pan in Ezekiel 4:3. He observes that the son of Shallum, or Meshelemiah, is called Zechariah in 1 Chronicles 26:2; if Zechariah was the chief branch of Shallum in David's day, then Mattithiah may have been so in the Chronicler's time — and the point he presses is that the duty of baking the sacred cakes for the meat-offerings was hereditary in this branch of Shallum's family. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the practical charge: Mattithiah was to take care that fine flour might be provided, that when occasion required they might make cakes in the pans.

The witnesses draw out how even so lowly a service was dignified by order. The baking of the offering-cakes was no casual chore but a fixed and inherited trust, committed to one named man of a known house, discharged "on good faith" as a sacred duty. That the same Korahite clan that kept the eastern gate should also furnish the overseer of the pans shows how the several offices ran in families, each house holding its portion of the work from father to son. Gill's bare identification, Keil's note on the trust, Ellicott's on the heredity, and Wesley's on the provisioning meet in one picture: the humblest task of the temple kitchen was drawn up into the same grand order as the guarding of the gates, a named man of a named family standing surety, generation after generation, that the bread of the offerings should be rightly made.
32:
Another Kohathite charge is named — the weekly baking of the shewbread — and the witnesses expound both the bread and the house that kept it. The reformed John Gill explains that twelve loaves were set every week before the ark upon the shewbread table, the old ones being taken away, and that the work of these Levites was to make this bread and get it ready each week; under the second temple, he adds from tradition, the family of Garmu was set over this task. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that to some of the Kohathites was committed the preparation of the shewbread, which had to be laid fresh on the table every sabbath, according to Leviticus 24:5-8.

Charles Ellicott ties the office to the family already traced. The Korahites, to whom Shallum and Mattithiah belonged, were a subdivision of the great clan of Kohath, so that the shewbread was kept by kinsmen of the gatekeepers and the pan-bakers. He explains the bread's names — "bread of the pile," and also "bread of the presence" — and its keeping: the Levites had to get it ready for the priests to lay it fresh on the golden table, after removing the old bread, every sabbath, as Leviticus 24:5-9 prescribes.

The witnesses feel the quiet significance of this perpetual bread. Twelve loaves for the twelve tribes lay continually before the LORD, renewed sabbath by sabbath, a standing sign of Israel set before the face of God; and its preparation, like every other charge in the chapter, was committed to a named house that made a business of it. Here again the division of labour that Matthew Henry admired is on display — the Levites to prepare, the priests to set on the table — and here again a single Kohathite family bore the trust from age to age. Gill's homely detail of the twelve loaves and the family of Garmu, Keil's grounding in the law of Leviticus, and Ellicott's note on the bread of the presence together make the verse a window into the settled rhythm of the sanctuary: the bread of God's presence never failing, baked each week by the appointed hands, laid fresh each sabbath on the golden table, the changeless token of a people kept continually before their God.
33:
The subscription to the Levitical section turns back to the singers, and the witnesses read their standing exemption as the badge of a wholly dedicated service. The reformed John Gill names them the singers, chief of the fathers of the Levites, the masters of the song who presided over that work. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that this verse and the next are subscriptions to the whole section from v.14 onward, and that since the enumeration of the Levites had begun with the singer-families, the subscription too begins with them: "these are the singers, heads of fathers' houses of the Levites," who, dwelling in the cells of the temple courts, were "free" — the word, they note with Rashi and Kimchi, meaning immune or released from every other ministry, that they might give themselves wholly to their own; and "day and night," they caution, is not to be pressed too literally.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, renders the harsh Hebrew to the same effect: "these are the minstrels, heads of Levitical families; in the temple cells they lived, exempt from all other charge; for day and night they were over them in the work." He too warns that it is hardly meant the service of song was literally unceasing, as the praise of Revelation 4:8 is unceasing, but only that the choristers were bound to a perpetually recurring service, the leaders for the time being living, like the chief warders, in the temple cells and presiding continually over the guilds of singers beneath them.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley expounds the exemption and the work with warmth. They lodged in the chambers, he says, that they might be ready whenever they were called to the service of God, and were freed from all other trouble and employment that they might wholly attend their proper task — the composing or ordering of sacred songs, the singing of them, or the teaching of others to sing them; and "day and night" he takes as continually, and particularly at the morning and evening hours of solemn service, so that God was continually praised, as it is fit he should be who is continually doing us good. The witnesses do not divide. Reformed, grammatical-historical, and wesleyan-arminian alike, they see in these singers a body lifted out of every other duty for the one work of praise, and housed within the temple that they might always be at hand, so that the courts of the LORD were never long without the sound of song.
34:
The Levitical register is sealed with a subscription, and the witnesses read it as the close of one great division and the turn toward another. The reformed John Gill takes "these chief fathers of the Levites were chief throughout their generations" to gather up all who have been named in the chapter, the heads of the Levitical houses. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it "these are the heads of the Levitical houses, according to their birth-rolls, heads," comparing 1 Chronicles 8:28, and reads the closing "these dwelt in Jerusalem" as a final remark upon all the Levites of the section, the proper names being regarded as chiefs under whom their numerous clansmen are subsumed.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley finds a providence in that closing clause. Upon their return from Babylon, he observes, the Levites were not suffered to choose their habitations in the country as others were, but were obliged to settle at Jerusalem, that they might constantly attend upon God's service there — the very concentration of the ministers about the temple being an arrangement of God for the good ordering of worship.

Keil and Delitzsch, looking forward, mark that the subscription also clears the way for the next movement of the book. With this verse the register of the restored community ends, and at v.35 begins the family of King Saul — a genealogy that has already appeared in 1 Chronicles 8:29-38 and is repeated here, they explain, to connect the following history of the kingship with the preceding genealogical lists, forming the introduction to the narrative of Saul's death in 1 Chronicles 10, which in turn makes the transition to the reign of David. The witnesses thus stand at a hinge: the long roll of the resettled priests, Levites, and gatekeepers is closed with a word of honour and of providence, the ministers gathered by God's own ordering about his house in Jerusalem; and the historian, having shown the worship of the restored nation set in order, now looks back to the throne, turning from the dwellers about the temple to the line of the first king, that he may bring his reader at last to David.
35:
The chapter turns abruptly from the temple to a family tree, repeating the pedigree of Saul, and the witnesses divide over why the repetition stands here at all. The reformed John Gill notes that Jehiel, "the father of Gibeon," is named here though his name was wanting in the parallel at 1 Chronicles 8:29, so that this register is in one point fuller than the other. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the plain purpose: the historian repeats Saul's genealogy that he might make way for the following history — the account of Saul's death and the rise of David.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, however, is not content that the repetition is wholly deliberate. These verses, he grants, are the very same as 1 Chronicles 8:29-38, there closing the genealogy of Benjamin, here introducing the story of Saul; yet, admitting that there are in the originals of these books some errors of transcribers, he is tempted to think this repetition arose from a copyist's slip — some scribe, having written "these dwelt in Jerusalem" at v.34, cast his eye on the same words at 1 Chronicles 8:28 and went on with what followed there, and, perceiving his mistake, was loth to blot his book and so let it stand.
