"Redundans non nocet," Henry quotes — redundancies do no harm. So a real question stands between the witnesses: is the second copy of Saul's line a designed bridge to the coming narrative, as Wesley and the grammatical-historical school hold, or an accidental duplication the writer chose not to erase, as Henry half suspects?

On one word the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens a textual difficulty. The verse calls Maachah the "wife" of Jehiel, but the Hebrew here reads a word meaning "his sister," while the Vulgate, Septuagint, Syriac, Arabic, Targum, and the parallel in 1 Chronicles 8:29 all have "wife"; and since Maachah could not be both sister and wife of Jehiel, one reading must be faulty. A single letter makes the whole difference, Clarke observes — one form spells "sister," another "wife" — and "wife" is most probably the true reading, as three ancient copies in his own possession attest. The witnesses thus open the Saul-genealogy on two uncertainties, one of purpose and one of text, yet agree that the pedigree stands here to carry the reader from the registers of the return back to the throne of the first king.
36:
The sons of Jehiel of Gibeon are numbered, and the witnesses, few here, attend to the meaning hidden in the ancient names. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply set the list in order — the firstborn Abdon, then Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, and Nadab, and after them Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth — carrying the register on toward Saul, and noting that this house "dwelt with their brethren at Jerusalem, over against their brethren," that is, near the other Benjamite families.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, reads the names as a small anthology of Israel's oldest theology. Zur, he explains, means "Rock," a divine title, and he compares the compounded names Pedahzur, "the Rock hath ransomed," and Zurishaddai, "the Rock is the Almighty" — pausing over the difficult word Shaddai, which he would connect either with a term for mountain or, better, with a root meaning "to pour out," so that it might signify "the giver of rain," comparing Joel 2:23. Even in a bare genealogy, he shows, the names of Saul's forefathers carry the memory of the God who was Israel's rock and provider.

Over one name Ellicott ventures a text-critical proposal that the witnesses record as a real possibility. The name Baal, he suggests, may have been compounded with the following Nadab to form a single name, Baal-nadab, "Baal is prince," on the analogy of Baal-gad and of Baal-hanan, "Baal is bounteous," in 1 Chronicles 1:49; and in that case, he adds, Ner would be out of its place in the list. It is a conjecture, not a certainty, and it touches the vexed matter of the Baal-names that run through the house of Saul — names later ages found troubling, since Baal was the title of the Canaanite god, yet which in these old Benjamite families seem to have meant no more than "lord" or "master," and may here be bound up with a word for "prince." The witnesses leave the verse, then, as a genealogy that is also a quiet lexicon: the fathers of Saul bearing names that speak of the Rock and the Almighty, and one name perhaps to be read afresh, so that even in the driest register the grammatical-historical eye finds both the faith and the difficulties of Israel's ancient speech.
39:
The line runs down at last to the first king, and the witnesses read the verse as the very point toward which the repeated genealogy has been bending. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the descent in order — Ner begat Kish, and Kish begat Saul, and Saul begat Jonathan, and Malchishua, and Abinadab, and Eshbaal, the four sons of the king whose fall the next chapter will relate. Here, they observe, the register reaches its purpose, for the whole family of Saul is repeated from the Benjamite lists only to bind the genealogies to the history that follows, introducing the narrative of Saul's death in 1 Chronicles 10 and so making the transition to the kingship of David.

The witnesses feel the weight of the names now assembled. Kish the father, Saul the king, and Jonathan the beloved son stand together in a single line, and with them Eshbaal — the Ishbosheth of the later history, whose Baal-name, like Meribbaal's after him, the books of Samuel would soften. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had read the whole repetition as the hinge between the genealogies and the story: what closed the account of Benjamin now opens the account of the monarchy, so that the reader is carried from the tribe to its king. Henry even wonders, with his usual candour, whether the repetition arose from a copyist's eye slipping from one "these dwelt in Jerusalem" to another, yet he is content to let it stand, on the old rule that redundancies do no harm.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke likewise takes these verses simply as the repetition of the genealogy already given at 1 Chronicles 8:33, the same descent set down a second time to serve the coming narrative. The witnesses converge on the plain and solemn function of the verse. It brings the long registers of the book to their first royal name: the line of Benjamin, traced through Gibeon and Ner and Kish, arrives at Saul and his sons, and the reader stands on the threshold of the history proper — the death of Saul upon Gilboa and the passing of the kingdom to David, toward whom the Chronicler's whole design has all along been moving.
41:
The line is carried past Saul to the house of his grandson, and the witnesses attend both to the names and to a small liberty the translators have taken. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the descent: the son of Jonathan was Meribbaal, and Meribbaal begat Micah, and the sons of Micah were Pithon, and Melech, and Tahrea, and Ahaz — the family of the surviving line of Saul preserved through Jonathan's lame son, the Mephibosheth whom David had spared.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the last name with a scholar's scruple. "Ahaz," he observes, is added here by the translators from the parallel at 1 Chronicles 8:35, the word being wanting in the Hebrew of this verse; and though it is now distinguished from the text by being printed in italics, such liberties, he warns, should only be taken in a note, for it is too much to expect that every editor of a Bible will attend to the distinction, and in process of time the supplied word will be found incorporated with the text as though it belonged there. It is a caution about the very craft of transmission — how an honest editorial supplement may harden, over generations of copying, into a false part of Scripture.

Clarke then repeats what he and the other witnesses have held throughout this closing section: that v.35 and those following are a repetition of 1 Chronicles 8:29-38, to whose notes he refers the reader. The witnesses thus read the verse on two levels at once. On its face it preserves the posterity of Saul through Jonathan and Meribbaal and Micah, showing that the fallen king's line did not perish but ran on in a quiet family of Benjamin. Beneath that, Clarke's note on the italic "Ahaz" opens a window into the labour of the text itself — the two parallel registers compared, the gaps of the one filled cautiously from the other, and the standing danger that such repairs, left unmarked, might one day be mistaken for the original. So even the last names of the book's genealogies are handled by the grammatical-historical and wesleyan-arminian voices with the same double care they gave the first: to preserve the descent, and to keep honest the record of how it has come down.
43:
The register nears its end with a run of names in the sixth and seventh generations from Saul, and the witnesses note chiefly the small variations that mark the two parallel copies. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the descent: Moza begat Binea; and Rephaiah his son, Eleasah his son, Azel his son — the line of Saul's house carried on through several quiet generations of whom nothing but the names survives.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, marks the one point of difference from the parallel: Rephaiah, he observes, appears in the contracted form "Rapha" in 1 Chronicles 8. It is a slight thing, a name given at full length here and shortened there, but it is of a piece with the many small divergences the witnesses have tracked between the Chronicler's register and its counterpart — differences of spelling and contraction that show two copies of one document, each worn a little differently in the copying. The descent itself, Keil and Delitzsch note, runs in the tightening formula "his son, his son, his son," a straight unbroken line from Moza through Binea and Rephaiah and Eleasah to Azel, each generation named only to hand the succession on.

The witnesses read these bare names as the faithful preservation of a line that the world would otherwise have wholly forgotten. Binea, Rephaiah, Eleasah, Azel — men of no recorded deed, remembered only because they stood in the descent of Saul; yet the historian sets them down, name by name, that the house of the first king might be traced to its end. Keil and Delitzsch had explained why this register was repeated at all: to bind the coming history of the monarchy to the genealogies, and because it had been derived from a document in fuller and better preservation than the parallel in 1 Chronicles 8. The single variant Ellicott notes, Rephaiah for Rapha, is a token of that independent source; and the witnesses leave the verse as one more link in a chain drawn patiently toward its close, the obscure descendants of Saul kept in the sacred record with the same fidelity given to the priests and gatekeepers of the restored temple, so that not one house of Israel, not even the fallen royal line, should pass entirely out of remembrance.
44:
The chapter, and with it the long genealogies of the book, ends on the six sons of Azel, and the witnesses close their comment where the register closes. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the names in full: Azel had six sons, Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan — "these were the sons of Azel," the last leaves of the tree of Saul.

Charles Ellicott marks a deliberate abridgment at the very end. This register omits the sons of Eshek and Ulam that stand in the parallel at 1 Chronicles 8:38-40, and the omission, he argues, is not due to inadvertence; he compares the similar shortening of the list in 1 Chronicles 6:4-15 when it is repeated later in that same chapter, and concludes that the present omission is owing either to the design of the Chronicler or to a like omission already in his source. The writer, that is, has closed his roll of Saul's house a little short of the parallel, of set purpose and not by accident.

The witnesses, gathering up the whole of this repeated genealogy, read its close in the light of its purpose. Keil and Delitzsch had explained that the register was set down a second time to connect the following history of the kingship with the preceding lists, and that its very deviations from 1 Chronicles 8 show it drawn from a fuller and better-preserved document. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, more cautiously, had wondered whether the repetition owed something to a transcriber's eye slipping from one "these dwelt in Jerusalem" to another, yet was content to let it stand, "redundancies do no harm"; and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke had simply named it a repetition of the earlier passage. So the book's genealogies end, on the six sons of a man otherwise unknown, with the witnesses divided only over whether the second copy is wholly by design or partly by accident, but united in this: that the line of Saul is here brought to its close precisely so that the historian may now leave the registers behind and turn, in the chapter that follows, to the death of the first king and the raising up of David, the true subject toward which every name in these nine chapters has been tending.
